content.text: The action space is the complete set of operations an agent can perform in a given run. It is not just a list of tools — it is a classification of those tools by their consequences. A
call leaves nothing changed and is safe to retry; a

call has an irreversible external effect. Understanding these distinctions before designing an agent's tool inventory is the foundation of safe agent operation: an agent can only cause harm it has the capability to cause, and removing unnecessary capabilities eliminates risk before the first line of agent logic runs.

attrs.alt: Action space — four classes of actions by reversibility and side effects

content.text: Every tool call falls into one of four classes. The class determines whether it is safe to retry, whether it requires explicit confirmation, and how the loop should handle failures:
— read-only actions have no consequences. They can be retried on failure, re-run on the same inputs, and are never a source of harm.
Class 2 is safe under version control
— writes to a git-tracked file are reversible with
. This classification holds only when the write is tracked; writes to untracked files or databases without transactions are closer to Class 3.
— pushing to a remote branch is hard to undo without force-push and coordination. These actions should never happen without explicit confirmation or prior authorisation in the system prompt.
— "permanently deleted" means gone. Class 4 tools should require explicit confirmation, be logged, and be absent from inventories where the task does not require them.

Give each agent only the action classes it needs. A research agent that reads the codebase and summarises it has no reason to push to git. A documentation writer has no reason to run SQL. Removing capabilities eliminates risk before the model sees a single tool:

# Principle of least privilege: give the agent only the action classes it needs.

def build_read_only_inventory(all_tools: dict) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Return only the tool definitions whose action class is READ_ONLY.
    Use for agents that should observe but not modify.
    """
    return [
        defn for name, defn in all_tools.items()
        if ACTION_METADATA.get(name, ActionMeta(name, ActionClass.READ_ONLY, "")).action_class
        == ActionClass.READ_ONLY
    ]


def build_inventory_for_classes(
    all_tools: dict,
    allowed_classes: set[ActionClass],
) -> list[dict]:
    """Build a tool list restricted to the specified action classes."""
    return [
        defn for name, defn in all_tools.items()
        if ACTION_METADATA.get(name, ActionMeta(name, ActionClass.IRREVERSIBLE, "")).action_class
        in allowed_classes
    ]


# Examples:
# Read-only research agent:
read_only_tools = build_inventory_for_classes(
    ALL_TOOL_DEFS,
    {ActionClass.READ_ONLY},
)

# Code-writing agent (may write but not push or delete):
write_tools = build_inventory_for_classes(
    ALL_TOOL_DEFS,
    {ActionClass.READ_ONLY, ActionClass.REVERSIBLE_WRITE},
)

# Full-capability deployment agent (all classes, use with approval gate):
all_tools = build_inventory_for_classes(
    ALL_TOOL_DEFS,
    {ActionClass.READ_ONLY, ActionClass.REVERSIBLE_WRITE,
     ActionClass.HARD_TO_REVERSE, ActionClass.IRREVERSIBLE},
)
produces a filtered tool list. Pass this to
— start with a read-only inventory and add write classes only when the task demonstrably requires them.
— in a multi-agent system, the investigator agent can be read-only while only the deployment agent has Class 3 access. Compromise of the investigator cannot cause a push.

For actions that are hard or impossible to reverse, a confirmation gate — called before the tool executes — prevents the model from proceeding without explicit approval. If denied, the gate returns an error result so the model can choose a different path:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def dispatch_with_confirmation(
    name: str,
    input: dict,
    auto_confirm_classes: set[ActionClass],
    confirm_fn,
) -> str:
    """
    Dispatch a tool call. For Class 3–4 actions, call confirm_fn before
    executing. If confirmation is denied, return an error result so the
    model can choose a different approach.

    confirm_fn(name, input) -> bool: returns True to proceed, False to abort.
    """
    meta = ACTION_METADATA.get(name)
    if meta and meta.action_class not in auto_confirm_classes:
        approved = confirm_fn(name, input)
        if not approved:
            return (
                f"ACTION DENIED: The operator did not approve {name!r}. "
                f"Choose a different approach that does not require this action."
            )

    return _tool_impls[name](**input)


def cli_confirm(name: str, input: dict) -> bool:
    """Interactive confirmation for terminal-based agents."""
    print(f"\nREQUEST: {name}({input})")
    answer = input("Approve? [y/N] ").strip().lower()
    return answer == "y"


# In the loop:
AUTO_CONFIRM = {ActionClass.READ_ONLY, ActionClass.REVERSIBLE_WRITE}

# For Class 3–4 tools, always ask before proceeding.
result = dispatch_with_confirmation(
    name=block.name,
    input=block.input,
    auto_confirm_classes=AUTO_CONFIRM,
    confirm_fn=cli_confirm,
)
confirm_fn is a parameter, not a hardcoded prompt
— this lets the loop work in interactive (CLI), automated (webhook approval), or test (always-approve) contexts without changing the dispatch logic.
Denied actions return an error result, not an exception
tool result keeps the loop running. The model sees the denial and must choose a different approach; it is not left in an undefined state.
— read and reversible-write actions do not need interactive confirmation. Only Class 3–4 gate on the confirm function.
Expanding the Action Space by Phase

Some agents start with a restricted action space and gain access to more powerful actions only after completing an earlier phase. This is an explicit safety pattern: the model cannot push or deploy until it has first investigated and implemented under a more restricted inventory:

# Action space expansion by phase:
# Some agents start with a read-only inventory and earn write access
# only after a verification phase confirms their plan is sound.

@dataclass
class PhaseSpec:
    name: str
    allowed_classes: frozenset[ActionClass]
    description: str


PHASES = [
    PhaseSpec(
        name="investigate",
        allowed_classes=frozenset({ActionClass.READ_ONLY}),
        description="Read and understand the codebase.",
    ),
    PhaseSpec(
        name="implement",
        allowed_classes=frozenset({ActionClass.READ_ONLY, ActionClass.REVERSIBLE_WRITE}),
        description="Implement the change.",
    ),
    PhaseSpec(
        name="deploy",
        allowed_classes=frozenset({
            ActionClass.READ_ONLY,
            ActionClass.REVERSIBLE_WRITE,
            ActionClass.HARD_TO_REVERSE,
        }),
        description="Push and deploy the change.",
    ),
]


def run_phased_agent(task: str, all_tool_defs: dict) -> str:
    """
    Agent loop that restricts the action space by phase.
    The model gains access to more powerful tools as it advances.
    """
    result = ""
    for phase in PHASES:
        tools = build_inventory_for_classes(all_tool_defs, phase.allowed_classes)
        phase_task = f"Phase: {phase.name} — {phase.description}\n\nOriginal task: {task}"

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=tools,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": phase_task}],
        )
        result = _extract_text(response)
        # In a real loop, check criteria before advancing to the next phase

    return result

Phase-gated action spaces are the agent equivalent of staged deployments. The model earns write access by completing a read-only phase first. Any failure in the read-only phase is contained to Class 1 — no state has changed.

After a run, the audit log tells you exactly which action classes were exercised. Verifying that no Class 4 actions were taken in a task that did not require them confirms the agent stayed within its intended scope:

import datetime
from dataclasses import dataclass, field


@dataclass
class ActionRecord:
    """One entry in the agent's action audit log."""
    tool: str
    input: dict
    action_class: str
    result_snippet: str
    ts: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat())


_audit_log: list[ActionRecord] = []


def audited_dispatch(name: str, input: dict) -> str:
    result = _tool_impls[name](**input)
    meta = ACTION_METADATA.get(name)
    _audit_log.append(ActionRecord(
        tool=name,
        input=input,
        action_class=meta.action_class.name if meta else "UNKNOWN",
        result_snippet=str(result)[:300],
    ))
    return result


def audit_summary() -> dict:
    """Count calls by action class for post-run review."""
    counts: dict[str, int] = {}
    for record in _audit_log:
        counts[record.action_class] = counts.get(record.action_class, 0) + 1
    return counts


# After the agent run:
# summary = audit_summary()
# => {"READ_ONLY": 12, "REVERSIBLE_WRITE": 3, "HARD_TO_REVERSE": 1}
# Verify no IRREVERSIBLE actions were taken if the task did not require them.
Log action class, not just tool name
— the audit summary groups by class so it is easy to verify at a glance that no irreversible actions were taken in a read-only task.
— tool results can be large. Logging the first 300 characters is enough to diagnose unexpected behaviour without inflating the audit log.
list is in memory during the run. For production agents, write it to a persistent store (database, append-only file) so it is available for post-run review.

The action space is the complete set of operations available to an agent, classified by their reversibility and side effects. The four classes — read-only, reversible write, hard-to-reverse, and irreversible — determine retry safety, confirmation requirements, and risk level. Apply the principle of least privilege: give each agent only the action classes its task requires. Use confirmation gates for Class 3–4 actions so they cannot execute without explicit approval. For complex tasks, phase-gate the action space — start read-only, expand to write classes only when the read phase is complete. Audit every run to verify that only the expected action classes were exercised.
